How It Was Generated / What It Means: Sequential unique respondent ID, 1 to N.
The latent persona used to generate this respondent. Kept here for teaching/validation only; should be DROPPED before running an unsupervised clustering exercise so clustering is genuinely blind.
Q1. Drawn independently of segment with realistic population proportions.
Q2. Drawn independently of segment.
Q3. Probability of seniority increases for AI Champion / Transformation Leader segments.
Q4. Drawn independently of segment.
Q5. Drawn independently of segment.

Q8. Strongly tied to segment: Champions/Leaders skew toward frequent use, Resisters toward never/rarely.
Q9. Loosely tied to each respondent's Change Fatigue (CFAT) latent score.
Q10. Tied to a blend of each respondent's Communication (COMM) and Leadership (LEAD) latent scores.
Q11. Tied to each respondent's Training Quality (TRAIN) latent score; also used as a multiplier in the Training_Quality_Score composite.

Q12. Lognormal draw, location parameter set per segment (Champions highest, Resisters lowest). ~0.8% of rows are forced into an extreme 160-220 hour range as deliberate outliers. Right-skewed by construction (skew ~8.5).
Q58 pair 1 (Untrustworthy-Trustworthy). Built from a blend of TRUST/PU/PEOU latent scores plus independent noise.
Q58 pair 2 (Threatening-Empowering). Built from a blend of TRUST/ENG latent scores plus independent noise.
Q58 pair 3 (Confusing-Intuitive). Built from PEOU latent score plus independent noise.
Q59. Each respondent's segment has a distinct weighting over the 5 factors; ranks derived from segment weight plus small random perturbation.
Q59, same mechanism as Rank_Training.
Q59, same mechanism.
Q59, same mechanism. Digital Resisters weight this highest on average.
Q59, same mechanism.
Q60. Segment-specific probability distribution over the 5 reaction options (e.g. Resisters skew heavily toward 'avoid using it').
Q63, pre-coded into 7 themes for this synthetic version (a real deployment would code free text post-hoc). Segment-specific probability distribution; used as an association-rule input.
Q13-Q15. Each item = LEAD latent score + independent item-level noise, binned to 1-5.

Category: Survey item (Job Security Concern, negative pole)
How It Was Generated / What It Means: Q48-Q49. Derived from JSEC latent score; higher = more job-security fear.
Q50. Derived directly from COLLAB latent score.
Q51 ('siloed within IT'). Derived from (100 - COLLAB latent score), i.e. already reverse-coded at generation; no further inversion needed for composite scoring.
Q52-Q53. Derived from INFRA latent score.
Q54-Q55. Derived from ENG latent score.
Q56-Q57. Derived from DCONF latent score.

Q61-Q62. NOT derived from a separate latent construct; instead built from a weighted blend of TRUST, PU, ENG (positive) and RES, ANX (negative), reflecting intent as a downstream behavioural consequence.
Mean of LEAD(3) + MGMT(2) + COMM(2) items, rescaled 0-100.
Mean of TRAIN(3) items, rescaled 0-100, weighted by a behavioural multiplier from Training_Received (1.0 structured, 0.7 one-time, 0.4 informal, 0.1 none).
Mean of TRUST(3) items, rescaled 0-100.
Mean of RES(3) + JSEC(2) items, rescaled 0-100. Higher = more resistant.
Mean of CFAT(2) items, rescaled 0-100. Higher = more fatigued.
Mean of DCONF(2) + DLIT(2) items, rescaled 0-100.
0.75 x mean(TRUST_3, INTENT_1, INTENT_2 rescaled) + 0.25 x Prior_AI_Experience (rescaled), plus independent composite-level noise. This is the direct input used to derive AI_Adopter_Flag.
Mean of TRUST(3) + reverse-coded ANX(2) + PU(3) + PEOU(2) items, rescaled 0-100.
0.8 x mean(INFRA(2), DCONF(2), DLIT(2) rescaled) + 0.2 x percentile rank of AI_Usage_Hours_Monthly.

Mean of Leadership_Readiness_Score, Employee Readiness sub-score (ENG+INTENT), CULT score, and INFRA score, plus an independent Normal(0, 15) composite-level noise term so the target is not a deterministic function of its visible predictors. This is the primary continuous dependent variable for regression.

Derived probabilistically (not a hard median split) from a logistic function of standardised Behavioural_Adoption_Intent_Score plus its own noise term, then a random draw against that probability. This creates a soft, realistic decision boundary: most but not all high-BAIS respondents are coded 1, and most but not all low-BAIS respondents are coded 0.

1 for the 25 respondents (2.08% of the sample) deliberately given internally inconsistent answers (see noise explanation). Provided so the data-cleaning exercise can locate and discuss these rows; would not exist in a real raw survey export and should be dropped or used only for the cleaning write-up, not as a model feature.
